sessions.details: Recorded 1980-10-15, first TX 07 November 1980. No known commercial release.
recorded 1970-01-21 (for BBC World Service Rhythm & Blues programme), first TX 1970-02-21 . No known commercial release.
recorded 1972-04-11 (Peel Session #4), first TX (on Top Gear) 1972-04-28. No known commercial release.

track_listing.track: Jetzt Kommt Die Flut (Here Comes The Flood)

track_listing.details: LP-Never Turn Your Back On A Friend; MCA

transcript.text: ...unless their journey is absolutely essential. And that's the news on Radio 1 at 2 minutes past 10.
On 275 and 285, also in stereo on VHF, this is National Radio 1.
Well hello there. This is TV on the radio, Thomas the Vance, and welcome once again to the Friday Rock Show.
Tonight we have a repeat of a session that you really seem to enjoy first time around.
It's by a band called Hibiscus.
And out of the archives, we feature two sets by Stone the Crows, one from 70 and one from 72.
We've got classic cuts from Hendrix, the Ozarks, Budgie and Rush, among others, and new releases by Witchvine.
Talking Heads, Trevor Rapin, Crocus, Rainbow and Big Daisy.
Not forgetting the teaser of the week, the Friday Night Connection.
And this evening, we start with this.
We call Witchvine from their album Stage Fright. The track was called Stage Fright.
10 minutes after 10, that track was for Graham Joyce of 65A Ballam Hill, London
And I must say that I agree with your choice there, because that's a good album
Prior to that, something by that monstrous band called Motorhead
Track from the album Overkill, the track was called No Class

Nick Cleverley, who lives in Salisbury near Wiltshire
Also Anne, who's in Belfast, she's Motorhead banger number 865
Rat wants a mention
via Motorhead.
He is in Reading.
John Mitchell of Hollywood in Northern Ireland
wants me to thank Motorhead for the great concert
that they put on over there.
I don't know how filthy Animal Taylor is at the moment.
I hope he's all right wherever he is.

who is in Whitfield in Dundee,

It's been released as a single.

But my advice to you would be to buy the album because it's a monster.
90 Minutes After 10, and before that you heard a real classic,
Jimi Hendrix from the album Are You Experienced?
The track was Foxy Lady.
And it was for a foxy lady whose name is Helen Bonner.
Well, according to John Honeyman, who lives in Bassett in Southampton.
He's a foxy lady.
John also wants me to mention all the hard-working electronic students at Southampton University.
Who are you trying to kid? Hard-working down there? Never.
Seems I played a wrong track last week.
I'll take your word for it, because a few letters came in telling me that I did not play Slogans by Steve Hackett.
I actually played the track called Time to Get Out.
If I did play the wrong track, I apologise, but the artist remains the same.
Way back last November, actually on the 7th of November,
we introduced a band to the airways by the name of Hibiscus,
and we got a lot of favourable reaction via your letters,
so we thought we'd put the session back on the air.
So here are Hibiscus and White Knight,
talented fella from South Africa, based in this country now,
Trevor Rabin, and a track from his album which is called Wolf.
The track was called She's Easy.
And before that you heard our session band,
Hibiscus, H-I-B-I-S-U-S, and White Knight.
It's 10.31 on the Friday Rock Show,
and here's the first of our archive sets tonight
by a band called Stone The Crows.
They were formed in 1969 in Scotland
and really, really, believe me, featured some amazing talent.
The lady that sings with them is still around, thank God.
Her name is Maggie Bell.
The lead guitarist unfortunately died.
I'll tell you how, just in case you don't know later.
His name was Les Harvey, and he had so much talent.
Also in the band was James Duar, or Jim Duar,
who now, of course, is with Robin Trier.
I was listening to these tracks this afternoon,
and they're ace.
They really are.

from May 1970.

the talent of the late Les Harvey
who was killed, I believe,
on the 4th of May 1972
when he was electrocuted
on stage in Swansea.
And it's said that
the reason he was electrocuted
was that somebody
but he and the audience had gone behind the stage
and probably just for the sheer heck of it and for being very foolish
had pulled the earth wire out of his amplifier.
So maybe the next time you think about mucking about with somebody's equipment,
please don't.
He was a great guitarist.
Maggie Bell was the lead vocalist, also in the band at that time.
You had Colin Allen on the drums and John McGuinness on the keyboards.
More from them later.
It's 18 minutes to 11 on the Friday Rock Show.
